coredata.dc:title: UV-induced oxidase activity of carbon dots in visible UVA dosage, Escherichia coli quantification and bacterial typing
coredata.dc:creator: Zhao, Dan
Liu, Huan
Zhang, Changpeng
Xiao, Xincai
He, Zhike
coredata.dc:description: Ultraviolet (UV) light and foodborne pathogenic bacteriais are an important risk to the environment's safety. They endanger human health, and also lead to outbreaks of infectious disease, posing great threats to global public health security, national economy, and social stability. The appearance of carbon dot (CD) nanozymes offers a new perspective to solve the problems of detection of UV light and pathogenic bacteria in environment. This paper reports the preparation of CDs with dual enzyme-like activities (superoxide dismutase activity and UV-induced oxidase activity). The product can catalyze the oxidation of the substrate 3, 3′, 5, 5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) under UV light (365 nm) to achieve rapid color development. Based on the excellent fluorescence properties of CDs, the colorimetric-fluorescence dual-channel real-time detection of UVA dose was realized, the mechanism underlying the catalytic oxidation of TMB by UV-induced oxidase CDs was also investigated. Furthermore, a portable CDs-TMB-PA hydrogel was prepared which could realize the real-time monitoring of UV in outdoor environment with the assistance of smartphone. Based on the pH dependency of the CD nanozymes and specific glycolytic response of the pathogenic bacteria Escherichia coli (E. coli) O157:H7, the direct, simple, quick, and sensitive typing and detection have been realized. This research offers new perspectives for studying CD nanozymes and their applications in UV and bacterial detection, demonstrating the remarkable potential of CD nanozymes in detecting environmental hazards.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Photocatalysis
Carbon dot nanozymes
Ultraviolet A dose
Portable gel
Escherichia coli O157:H7
Visual detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Optimization of the synthesis parameters for dual enzyme active CD nanozymes
Characterization of dual enzyme active CD nanozymes
UV-induced catalytic performance of the CDs and their underlying mechanism
Performance of the portable gel for smartphone-assisted UVA detection
Glucose metabolism-triggered E. coli O157:H7 detection and lactose-based bacterial typing

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Fundamental Research Funds for South-Central Minzu University
National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was funded by the Fundamental Research Funds for South-Central Minzu University ( CZY22003 ) and the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 22174102 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Ultraviole-visible (UV–Vis) spectra of TMB (without UVA irradiation, red), TMB and CDs (without UVA irradiation, green), TMB (after UVA irradiation, blue), and TMB and CDs (after UVA irradiation, purple). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
Inhibition of catechol autoxidation rate by different concentrations of CDs.

(a) UV–Vis absorption spectra and emission spectra of the CDs (insets are the photos of the solution under visible and UV-excitation). (b) Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images, the inserts are HRTEM images (left), see Fig. S9 for single diagram details.
Discoloration of TMB induced by different UV wavelengths in the presence or absence of CDs.
Effect of solution pH on the UVA-induced catalytic oxidation of TMB by CDs with an irradiation time of 30 s (insets are images of the TMB solutions at different pH values).

Mechanism of UV-induced TMB oxidation. (a) Effects of different enzyme active substances on TMB oxidation were simulated under UVA irradiation. EDTA-2Na, IPA, Trp, CuCl2, and BQ were used as h+, •OH, 1O2, e−, and O2 •− scavengers, respectively. (b) Proposed overall mechanism of UV-induced TMB oxidation.

(a) The effect of UVA irradiation time on the absorption spectra of CDs-TMB solution. (b) Linear relationship between absorbance changes (A652) and UVA dose from 7.8 to 78 J/m2. (c) Emission spectra of the CDs-TMB solution after different UVA irradiation times. (d) Standard working curve of fluorescence quenching efficiency versus irradiation dose, see Fig. S10 for single diagram details.

(a) Effect of different UVA doses on the degree of color development of CDs-TMB-PA gel/TMB-PA gel (control group: bottom row of the TMB-PA gel). (b) Fitted curves between UVA doses and R/G values. (c) Red-green-blue analysis of the generated images using a color identifier app. (d) Real-time changes in the color of the CDs-TMB-PA gel on rainy and sunny days and color changes of the gel at different times of the same day, see Fig. S11 for single diagram details. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
(a) Logarithmic plot of the absorbance at 450 nm versus E. coli O157:H7 concentration. (b) Calibration curve for the detection of E. coli O157:H7 using a smartphone for sensing.
Specificity of the colorimetric sensor for different bacterial types in the presence of lactose (n = 3).
Schematic diagram of the photo-triggered CDs-TMB colorimetric sensor for UVA detection with aqueous solution (fluorescence quenching) and hydrogel media (CDs-TMB gel).
Schematic diagram of the principle of bacterial typing by enzyme-specific metabolism of lactose by different bacteria.

head.ce:title: UV-induced oxidase activity of carbon dots in visible UVA dosage, Escherichia coli quantification and bacterial typing

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Pharmaceutical Sciences, South-Central Minzu University, Wuhan, 430074, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Molecular Sciences, Wuhan University, Wuhan, 430072, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Molecular Sciences, Wuhan University, Wuhan 430072, P. R. China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Wuhan Research Center for Infectious Diseases and Cancer

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Wuhan Research Center for Infectious Diseases and Cancer, Chinese Academy of Medical Sciences, Wuhan, 430071, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. College of Chemistry and Molecular Sciences, Wuhan University, Wuhan, 430072, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Molecular Sciences

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Ultraviolet (UV) light and foodborne pathogenic bacteriais are an important risk to the environment's safety. They endanger human health, and also lead to outbreaks of infectious disease, posing great threats to global public health security, national economy, and social stability. The appearance of carbon dot (CD) nanozymes offers a new perspective to solve the problems of detection of UV light and pathogenic bacteria in environment. This paper reports the preparation of CDs with dual enzyme-like activities (superoxide dismutase activity and UV-induced oxidase activity). The product can catalyze the oxidation of the substrate 3, 3′, 5, 5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) under UV light (365 nm) to achieve rapid color development. Based on the excellent fluorescence properties of CDs, the colorimetric-fluorescence dual-channel real-time detection of UVA dose was realized, the mechanism underlying the catalytic oxidation of TMB by UV-induced oxidase CDs was also investigated. Furthermore, a portable CDs-TMB-PA hydrogel was prepared which could realize the real-time monitoring of UV in outdoor environment with the assistance of smartphone. Based on the pH dependency of the CD nanozymes and specific glycolytic response of the pathogenic bacteria Escherichia coli (E. coli) O157:H7, the direct, simple, quick, and sensitive typing and detection have been realized. This research offers new perspectives for studying CD nanozymes and their applications in UV and bacterial detection, demonstrating the remarkable potential of CD nanozymes in detecting environmental hazards.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Compared with natural enzymes, artificial nanozymes have obvious advantages such as strong catalytic activity, low synthesis cost, and good stability [1,2]. They are ideal candidates for developing multifunctional optical sensing platforms for different targets. Among them, carbon dot (CD) nanozymes exhibited excellent optical properties, enzyme-like activity, easy mass production, low synthesis cost, and easy adjustment. They are regarded as highly promising and sensitive dual-channel optical probes that could be applied in the specific and sensitive detection of biochemical materials, including hydrogen peroxide [3,4], glutathione [5], and uric acid [6].

Preparing CD nanozymes and further investigation of their catalytic properties are the basis for their application in sensing systems. To design and prepare CD nanozymes with long wavelength-emitting properties, excellent biocompatibility, and specific enzyme activity is the hotspot of current researches. For the present, most reported CD nanozymes are blue-emitting materials [7,8] and the established sensing platform can be easily affected by background noises. The metal ion doping into CDs for the catalytic activity negatively affected the optical properties of CDs [9], like blue-shifting of the emission wavelength, lower fluorescence quantum yields, and even set great risk to the environment during in vivo detection because of the potential metal ion leakage. In the present, researchers are working on the CD nanozymes with multiple enzyme-like activities [10,11], which could avoid the drawbacks of single enzyme-like activity, such as insufficient catalytic efficiency, limited availability of substrate type and concentration, and single recognizable catalytic condition. The researches on the subspecies of nanozymes are uneven. Photo-responsive nanozymes can induce or enhance the biocatalytic activity of photo-nanozymes under the external stimulation of electromagnetic waves of different wavelengths [12], such as TiO2, ZnO, Ag3PO4, CDs, photoactivated COF, etc [13,14]. It was discovered that photoinduced nanozymes usually possess advantages like higher activity, photo-adjustability, and use of oxygen as green oxidant [15]. Recently, researchers reported the application of UV-induced oxidase activity of P-NCDs into the detection of Hg2+ [16]. Therefore, the development of long-wavelength-emitting, non-metal-doping and photo-induced active CD nanozymes and their application into biochemical detection fields, are of great research and application values.

Ultraviolet (UV) light is an important detection target associated with environmental safety. According to its radiation energy, UV light can be further classified into three types: UVA (315–400 nm), UVB (280–315 nm), and UVC (200–280 nm) [17]. Among them, UVA exhibits the strongest ability in penetrating the human skin and accounts for the largest percentage (90%) of UV light in daily environment. Excessive exposure to UV light may lead to skin aging lesions and increased risk of skin cancer [18,19]. The real-time and quantitative detection of UV radiation doses in outdoor environment is vital for human health. In general, UV detection is achieved using the photoelectric sensing method [20,21], where the signal is converted from UV intensity to a radiation dose using microcomputing chips [22], but this method requires spectral calibration of the UV power meter of the photodetector, is unsuitable for daily real-time monitoring of UV light, and is not user-friendly, particularly for inexperienced operators. Compared with the photoelectric sensing method, optical sensing possesses advantages such as simple equipment, scalability, and user-friendliness, making it the hotspot for outdoor UV detection. Recently, Zhang et al. discovered that the chromogenic substrate 3, 3′, 5, 5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) exhibits a typical color change response to UVB and UVC, thereby realizing the visual dosimetry of UV light [23]. However, this method did not have satisfactory sensitivity. The emergence of photo-induced catalytic CD nanozymes has provided excellent catalysts and fluorescence probes for the fast, dual-channel, and sensitive detection of UV light. Compared with the single-signal output mode, this colorimetric-fluorescence dual-signal output mode can provide richer information, and its built-in cross-reference, self-correction, and self-verification functions decrease environmental and operational interference. The loading of nanozymes on a portable gel can help achieve the naked-eye or smartphone-assisted identification of UV light, providing new strategies for the point-of-care testing (POCT) of UV light in resource-limited areas and outdoors.

Similar to UV light, food-borne pathogens are also important detection target for environment safety. Pathogenic bacteria are not only dangerous to human health but also a crucial factor associated with the outbreak of infectious diseases, setting great threat to global public health security, national economy, and social stability [24]. Escherichia coli (E. coli), particularly enterohemorrhagic E. coli (E. coli O157:H7), is widespread in nature and is the most common cause for food-borne disease outbreaks [25,26]. Considering the increasing serious food and environmental safety problems at present, the development of rapid, sensitive, and reliable detection methods for E. coli O157:H7 is vital for infectious disease control and precision medicine. Compared with the time-consuming bacterial culture and nucleic acid detection methods that heavily rely on professional equipment and operators, optical sensing possesses advantages such as simple equipment, scalability, and user-friendliness. Therefore, it is an important breakthrough if the detection of food-borne pathogenic bacteria can be done by inexperienced operators. Since, the optical signal unit for sensing system should be highly sensitive, CD nanozymes with dual-channel output signals are a good choice. Different from time-consuming and laborious probe labeling identification, the development of a specific non-labelled bacterial detection strategy at the cellular level is highly significant. Liu et al. used pH-sensitive CDs as fluorescence probes to realize the rapid detection and quantification of two bacteria, i.e., Staphylococcus aureus and E. coli, based on the different abilities of bacterial enzymes to produce acid from glucose and lactose [27]. Compared with fluorescence-based CDs detection, CD nanozymes with the ability to detect catalytic amplification can be used for developing a highly sensitive and visual non-labelled detection strategy for pathogenic bacteria.

This paper reports the applications of prepared yellow-emitting CD nanozymes with catalytic activity as fluorescence probe to realize dual-channel sensing (fluorescence and naked-eye colorimetry) of UVA light, based on the catalytic ability of CD nanozymes for the rapid color development of TMB. Furthermore, CDs and the substrate (TMB) were co-encapsulated into a portable gel for naked-eye visualization and to prepare a smartphone-assisted quantitative portable sensor for timely and outdoor (on-site) monitoring of UV light. Based on the pH-dependent catalytic activity of CDs and the specificity of the acidification properties of glycolysis in E. coli O157:H7, a sensitive and specific visual detection strategy was developed. This study provides new perspectives for expanding the catalytic performance of CDs and the POCT of environmental hazards, such as UV light and pathogenic bacteria.

The synthesis method was slightly modified from the previous work [28] as follows: 0.03 g 4-ASA and 0.05 g PEI (MW.10000) were dissolved in 25 mL of water, the pH of the mixed solution was adjusted to 2.5, and then transferred to a PTFE-lined reactor and passed 3 min of N2, and the reaction was carried out at 200 °C for 3 h.

Overall, 3 mM TMB was dissolved in DMSO for subsequent analysis. The CDs solution, TMB solution was mixed with 20 mM PBS (pH = 3). The mixture was illuminated with UV light sources of different wavelengths and photographs showing color changes were collected and UV tests were performed outdoors.

The UVA dose was measured by TMB solution, and the absorbance of oxTMB at 652 nm was measured by mixing the solution containing CDs and TMB in a cuvette with UVA (365 nm, 5 W) irradiation at different times. Similarly, PBS (pH = 3), CDs (3 mg mL−1), and TMB (3 mM) were homogeneously mixed. The fluorescence spectra were then measured with 390 nm excitation wavelength by irradiating at different durations of UVA.

PVA and SA were dissolved in PBS (20 mM) at pH = 3. 10 % PVA was mixed 7:3 with 1 % SA solution, and TMB and CDs solutions were added to the mixed solution (only TMB solution was added to the control group, no CDs solution was added), which was injected into the container, and CDs-TMB-PA gel was obtained by repeated freeze-thawing three times. The prepared gels were sealed in self-sealing bags and stored at 4 °C.

E. coli O157:H7 strains were scraped from nutrient agar plates and activated. Before further manipulation, the cultures were incubated with LB medium in a rotary shaker at 37 °C and 180 r min−1 for 12 h. The cultures were then diluted to 101–108 CFU mL−1 and subsequently incubated in peptone water medium containing 20 mM glucose for 8 h at 37 °C. After that, CDs and TMB were added to the suspension, irradiated with UVA for 3 min, and then 1 M H2SO4 was added to observe and record the absorbance at 450 nm and the color change of their solutions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of the synthesis parameters for dual enzyme active CD nanozymes
ce:section.ce:para: Yellow-green-emitting CDs with catalytic activity were rapidly prepared via the hydrothermal route using 4-ASA and PEI as raw materials. Fig. 1 demonstrates that under UVA illumination, CDs could instantly oxidize the colorless substrate TMB into blue oxidation products. In contrast, UVA illumination did not change the color of the TMB solution without the presence of CDs, and the mere presence of CDs in the TMB solution did not lead to a color change without the illumination UVA. The characteristic peak of the TMB oxidation product at 652 nm was not observed in the presence of CDs without UVA irradiation, suggesting that CDs have UV-induced oxidase (OXD) activity. The effects of the polymerization degree of the passivator PEI, feeding ratio of the reaction materials, pH value of the precursor solution, and reaction time on the catalytic activity of the product were investigated. As illustrated in Fig. S1a, an increase in the molecular weight (600–10,000) of PEI gradually strengthened the activity of the prepared CDs in catalyzing TMB oxidation. Among cationic polymers, PEI is one of the most efficient passivators, which can improve fluorescence properties and photostability and decrease the toxicity of prepared CDs [29]. PEI with a higher molecular weight possesses more branches, beneficial for the complete reaction of raw materials and increases the sensitivity of the prepared CDs to UV light. As shown in Figs. S1b, c, and d, when pH = 2.5, the mass ratio of raw materials (PEI:4-ASA) = 5 : 3, and reaction time at 3 h, the fluorescence property and catalytic activity of prepared CDs reach optimal, with the quantum yield at 16.54 %. In addition, the kinetic parameters of the OXD activity of the prepared CDs were investigated under optimal conditions. With a fixed CDs concentration (3 mg mL−1) and varying TMB concentrations (1–5 mM), the Michaelis-Menten curves of the activity data were illustrated Fig. S2a. The relationship between 1/V and 1/[TMB] is shown in the Lineweaver-Burk plot (Fig. S2b), with the following regression equation: y = 150.66x + 135.96 (R2 = 0.9916). The maximum reaction rate (Vmax) and Michaelis-Menten constant (Km) were 7.61 μM s−1 and 1.28 mM, respectively.

SOD enzyme is a kind of enzyme that could scavenge O2 •−. Further studies showed that some CDs could exhibit similar activity as SOD enzyme. The experiments to inhibit the autoxidation of o-benzenetriol were used to assess the SOD enzyme activity of CDs [30]. Under an alkaline environment, o-benzenetriol could autoxidize rapidly, release O2 •− and produce colored intermediates. The presence of SOD enzyme could prevent the accumulation of intermediates, and thus the low value of absorbance of the intermediates means high SOD enzyme activity. As shown in Fig. 2 , when CDs concentration is at 0.4 mg mL−1, its inhibition rate to O2 •− reaches 68%. Even when the concentration is at only 0.025 mg mL−1, the inhibition rate is still 10 %, showing the excellent SOD enzyme activity of the prepared CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of dual enzyme active CD nanozymes
ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescence and UV–Vis spectroscopies were performed to investigate the optical properties of the prepared CDs. As shown in Fig. 3 a and Fig. S3a, the CDs solution is pale yellow and exhibits yellow-green fluorescence under UV light illumination, with an optimal excitation wavelength (λex) at 390 nm and emission wavelength (λem) at 525 nm. Two UV absorption peaks were observed in the UV–Vis spectrum of the prepared CDs. The peak at 258 nm was attributed to the π → π* transition on the sp2 orbit of the benzene ring, whereas the other peak at 385 nm was attributed to the n → π* transition on the CO/CN group caused by the excited defect state induced on the CDs surface. Fig. 3b and Fig. S3b show the morphology and size distribution of the prepared CDs. The prepared CDs were well dispersed, with uniform and normal size distribution and an average particle size of 3.50 nm. The HRTEM image of the CDs in the inset shows the clear lattice strips with lattice spacing of 0.20 nm. The hemolysis assay is a simple and reliable method to determine the hemocompatibility of nanomaterials. As shown in Fig. S4, compared with the negative and positive control groups, even in the presence of high-concentration CDs (625 μg mL−1), there were no changes in the morphology of red blood cells (RBCs), with a hemolysis rate at 3.3 %. In general, a rate lower than 5.0 % is often considered safe for biomedical applications [31]. These experimental results showed that CDs do not rupture and hemolyze RBCs under physiological conditions, confirming the excellent hemocompatibility of the prepared CDs. The FT-IR and XPS spectra results (in the supplementary materials) further prove the presence of amino, and phenolic hydroxyl functional groups of prepared CDs (Fig. S5 and Fig. S6).

ce:section.ce:section-title: UV-induced catalytic performance of the CDs and their underlying mechanism
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the differences in energy levels, UV light can be further classified into three types: UVA (315–400 nm), UVB (280–315 nm), and UVC (200–280 nm) [17]. We elucidated the effects of UV light with different energies (i.e., wavelengths) on the photocatalytic performance of the prepared CDs. Fig. 4 demonstrates that the color of the TMB solution changed from colorless to blue under UVB illumination, consistent with the findings of a previous study [23]. In the presence of CDs, all three UV light types can change the color of the TMB solution, where UVA exhibiting the strongest ability to induce CDs catalysis. The absorbance of the solution increases with the increase of illumination time. Therefore, the under a fixed light power, the illumination time can be converted into light dose, with potential application in the colorimetric detection of UVA based on the photocatalytic oxidation reaction of CDs-TMB.

As shown in Fig. 5 , the photocatalytic activity of CDs is closely associated with solution pH, and the optimal pH was proven to be 3. When the pH was 3, TMB was oxidized as oxTMB, with the maximum absorption wavelength at 652 nm and deep blue of solution color. In contrast, when the solution pH was ≥5, the solution was light blue or colorless. When the solution pH was 2, the solution changed from colorless to yellow-green (λmax = 450 nm). This phenomenon can be used to visualize pathogenic bacteria based on the adjustment of the photocatalytic activity of CDs caused by pH changes owing to sugar metabolism in the microorganisms.

UV-induced oxidation is generally associated with reactive oxygen species [32], including hydroxyl radicals (•OH), superoxide anions (O2 •−), singlet oxygen (1O2), and holes (h+). The mechanism by which UV-induced CDs catalyze TMB oxidation has been explored through intermediate scavenging experiments. Fig. 6 a demonstrates that when nitrogen gas was filled into the solution to remove oxygen, the absorbance of the chromogenic system significantly decreased indicating that the dissolved oxygen in the solution plays a vital role in catalyzing TMB oxidation. Since BQ can effectively scavenge O2 •− [33], the addition of BQ into the solution resulted in significant color weakness, suggesting that O2 •− is an effective active substance that participates in TMB oxidation. However, the addition of IPA [34], a •OH scavenger, did not lead to obvious changes in the absorbance of oxTMB, illustrating that •OH is not a major active substance in TMB oxidation. Similarly, the addition of Trp, a trapping agent for 1O2, did not lead to obvious changes in the absorbance of the system, proving that 1O2 is also not involved in catalyzing the oxidation process [35]. However, the addition of CuCl2 leads to an obvious decrease in absorbance, suggesting that photogenerated electrons (e−) play an essential role in the photocatalytic process [36]. Nevertheless, the addition of EDTA-2Na, an h+ eliminator, did not lead to changes in the absorbance of the solution, showing that photogenerated h+ are not the major oxide species [37]. Based on the findings of the above-mentioned experiments results, the mechanism underlying the UV-induced OXD of CDs was thus proposed. With the absence of CDs, only UVB with higher energy could induce O2 •– production, and the oxidation led to a color change in the solution. As shown in Fig. 6b, CDs possess UV-activated OXD activity when UVA illuminates on the CDs surface, and if the energy of the light is higher than the forbidden bandwidth of the CDs, the valence band e− of the CDs transit from the valence band to the conduction band, producing photogenerated e− and h+. Since phototropic e− is highly reductive and can combine with oxygen to form oxidizing active O2 •−. As a result, the substrate TMB is oxidized into blue-colored oxTMB.

Scheme 1 presents the colorimetric-fluorescence dual-channel sensing system for rapid UVA detection based on the UVA-induced nanozyme activity of the prepared CDs. Fig. 7 a and b demonstrate that under UVA illumination and in the presence of CDs (3 mg mL−1), the absorbance of the TMB solution (3 mM) enhances with the increase of illumination time, and the solution color changed from colorless to blue. The absorbance maintained a good linear relationship with the UVA dose, with linear equation was as y = 0.0093x + 0.1682, where y is the absorbance of the colorimetric sensor and x is the UVA dose. The regression coefficient (R2) is 0.9950 and linear range is 7.8–78 J/m2, The LOD (3σblank/S) was 0.24 J/m2, where σblank is the standard deviation of the blank sample signal (n = 11), and S is the slope of the calibration curve. With the increased irradiation time of UVA light, the color development degree of the substrate TMB by CDs’ catalytic oxidation gradually enhances. When stopping the light irradiation, the catalytic oxidation reaction to TMB also stops, unable to further accelerate the color change of TMB. Therefore, the irradiation time of UV light (i.e. UV dose) is correlated with the degree of TMB color development.

In addition, under UVA irradiation, the yellow-green emitting CDs exhibited high quantum yields and excellent photostability. However, with an increase in the UVA irradiation time, the fluorescence intensity of the CDs solution (in the absence of TMB) did not exhibit obvious changes, further suggesting the excellent photostability of the CDs (Fig. S7). Moreover, the fluorescence intensity of the CDs in the CDs-TMB system decreased with the increase of UVA irradiation time (Fig. 7c and d). Meanwhile, the active substances (i.e. superoxide anion) produced by CD nanozymes after the irradiation might also affect the fluorescence of CDs. Therefore, it was inferred that the fluorescence quenching phenomenon is the combined effects of internal filtration effect and superoxide anion. The changes in fluorescence quenching efficiency maintained a good linear relationship with the UVA dose. The linear equation was as follows: y = 0.0032x + 0.0790, with an R2 of 0.9940 and linear range of 7.8–91 J/m2, where y is the fluorescence quenching efficiency of the fluorescence sensor and x is the UVA dose. The LOD was defined as three times the relative standard deviation (RSD) of 11 times of analysis and was calculated as 3.9 J/m2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Performance of the portable gel for smartphone-assisted UVA detection
ce:section.ce:para: To meet the POCT standards of cost-effectiveness and accessibility, a portable photosensitive gel was prepared for outdoor monitoring of the UV dose based on the CDs-TMB photocatalytic system, which was designed for the public and inexperienced operators. The gel matrix materials were preferentially selected, and two gel types were prepared through different raw materials and synthesis methods to assess their mechanical properties and color development stability. The experiments revealed that the gel prepared by agarose exhibited poor mechanical properties. It was fragile, and exhibited rapid color fading, failing to meet the requirements of portability and stability as a senser. On the other hand, the PA gel prepared using PVA and SA as the raw materials exhibited good mechanical properties, good viscoelastic effect, easy portability, and color stability after color development. So this gel was used for further experiments. As shown in Fig. 8 a, TMB-encapsulated gel (TMB-PA) did not generate a color development reaction under UVA irradiation, but the gel encapsulating both TMB and CDs (CDs-TMB-PA) exhibited color changes from milky white to blue with an increase in the UVA exposure time, and the color went darker with the increase of light dose.

The smartphone with camera and imaging processing apps has been used to converse the gel color signal into UVA dosage for outdoor UVA monitoring. The gel color was firstly captured through smartphone camera, and then converted into digital values representing red, green, and blue (RGB) color patterns using the color picker app, as shown in Fig. 8c. The ratio of the red (R) and green (G) channels was calculated for UVA dose analysis. As shown in Fig. 8b, R/G and the UVA dose keeps a good linear relationship, which can be represented using two consecutive linear relationships: y = −0.0074x + 0.9488 (R2 = 0.9933) and y = −0.0013x + 0.6819 (R2 = 0.9913), with linear ranges of 0–39 and 39–390 J/m2, respectively.

Sunlight is a combination of visible light, infrared light, and a small amount of UV light, but the low-energy infrared and visible light do not induce the catalytic oxidation of TMB by CDs. As demonstrated in Fig. 8d, the color of CDs-TMB-PA gel changes into distinct blue color after 2 min's exposure in the sun on a sunny day (May 27, 2023, Wuhan, China), but it didn't show obvious change under cloudy weather (May 24, 2023, Wuhan, China). In addition, the exposure of the CDs-TMB-PA gel at different times of the same day led to changes in gel color, and and the color differed from the early morning to late afternoon. Therefore, the gel can be used for the real-time monitoring of changes in the UV dose in sunlight in an outdoor environment. The preparation of the CDs-TMB-PA gel extends the visual detection of UV from the aqueous phase to the solid phase, exhibiting distinctive advantages such as low cost, portability, sensitivity, rapidity, and easy signal interpretation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Glucose metabolism-triggered E. coli O157:H7 detection and lactose-based bacterial typing
ce:section.ce:para: Since bacteria can decompose sugars (such as glucose or lactose) and release acidic metabolites [38], so their existence usually decreases the pH value of the medium, which could be used to indicate the concentration of bacteria in the solution. Based on the above-mentioned experiments (Fig. 5), under the fixed UVA dose, the changes in pH values can affect the color development of the CDs-TMB system. With E. coli O157:H7 as the detection target and a 40 mM glucose solution as medium, the culture supernatant was collected after 8 h of cultivation, and 3 mg mL−1 CDs and 3 mM TMB were added into the solution. After 3 min of UVA irradiation, the visualization method and UV–Vis spectrophotometry were used for bacterial detection. The substance glucose in the medium can reduce the dimeric charge-transfer complex oxTMB (blue color), resulting in the gradual color fading of the solution. Then 1 M H2SO4 was added after UVA irradiation to terminate the reaction, and produce a stable yellow quinone-conjugated monomer structure (450 nm) [39], markedly improving the color development stability for further experiments. As shown in Fig. 9 a, as the bacterial concentration decreased from 5.0 × 106 to 1.0 × 103 CFU mL−1, the response signal (absorbance at 450 nm) also decreased. The response signals and bacterial concentrations keeps good linear relationship in the range of 5.0 × 106–1.0 × 103 CFU mL−1, which can be described by the equation y = 0.0883x + 0.2835, with a coefficient (R2) of 0.9954, where y is the absorbance of the solution at 450 nm and x is the logarithm of the bacterial concentration. E. coli can break down the glucose in the medium into pyruvate, which could be further degraded into acids (such as acetic acid) through the action of enzymes (such as pyruvate lyase), thus reducing pH value of the medium.

The different color signals represent different bacteria concentrations, which can be easily distinguished under naked-eye visualization (Fig. 9b). Smartphones can be used as endpoint readers to capture images and convert the color signals to digital values of RGB color patterns to accurately determine bacterial concentrations. The calibration curve between their R and E. coli O157:H7 concentrations can be expressed by the equation y = 9.628x + 175.76 (R2 = 0.9966), with a detection range of 101–108 CFU mL−1. Therefore, the E. coli O157:H7 detection and its concentrations can be easily facilitated through the combination of the CDs-TMB sensing system and the assistance of a smartphone, remarkably potential for applications in practical settings.

Similar to E. coli O157:H7, Bacillus subtilis, Pseudomonas aeruginosa, and methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus (MRSA) can effectively decrease the pH values of the solution in the presence of glucose, and exhibit a strong absorption peak at 450 nm (Fig. S8). So the application of glucose as a substrate cannot help to distinguish bacterial types.

Since different pathogenic bacteria show evident differences in the metabolic activity of different sugars, bacterial typing can be achieved based on the specific property of E. coli, because it can hydrolyze lactose via a specific galactosidase and release acid products, as illustrated in Scheme 2 . As shown in Fig. 10 , when culturing different pathogenic bacteria in a medium containing lactose, only E. coli O157:H7 changed the CDs-TMB system, whereas the other bacteria did not produce a similar phenomenon. This phenomenon can be used for visual bacterial typing. The mechanism of this typing process was investigated. First, the experiments results showed when the medium contained no glucose or lactose, the solution did not exhibit any color changes in the presence of all bacterial types, suggesting that the acid products generated during bacterial growth cannot affect the absorbance of the CDs-TMB system in the absence of sugar molecules. However, in the presence of lactose in the medium, E. coli can express β-galactosidase (β-gal), and hydrolyze lactose to galactose and glucose [40,41], which are subsequently converted to pyruvate via a metabolic process and further decomposed into acid substances such as acetic acid [42], decreasing the pH value of the medium. In contrast, other bacteria without β-gal cannot decompose lactose to produce acid products, unable to change the pH of the medium and thus failing to change the color of CDs-TMB system.

This study reports the prepared CDs with dual enzyme-like activities (SOD enzyme activity and UV-induced OXD activity) and elucidated the effects of synthesis parameters, UV light with different energies, and solution pH upon the photocatalytic activity of the prepared CDs. The mechanism by which UV-induced CDs catalyze TMB oxidation was investigated through intermediate scavenging experiments. Based on the UV sensitivity of the CDs, a colorimetric-fluorescence dual-channel detection system for UVA was established. Furthermore, a portable CDs-TMB-PA gel was prepared, and with the assistance of a smartphone, UV light was monitored in a practical environment. The pathogenic bacteria E. coli O157:H7 was typed and detected in a visual, simple, fast, and sensitive manner based on the pH dependency of the CD nanozymes and the specific glycolytic response of E. coli O157:H7. This study findings provide novel perspectives for studying CD nanozymes and their applications in the detection of UV and bacteria, showing the remarkable potential of the prepared CD nanozymes in detecting environmental hazards.

Dan Zhao: Funding acquisition, Investigation, Project administration, Writing – original draft. Huan Liu: Data curation, Investigation, Methodology, Formal analysis. Changpeng Zhang: Validation, Data curation, Investigation. Xincai Xiao: Conceptualization, Software, Validation. Zhike He: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: There are no conflicts of interest to declare.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was funded by the Fundamental Research Funds for South-Central Minzu University (CZY22003) and the National Natural Science Foundation of China (22174102).
